questions.prompt: What does a @DataProvider supply to a test?
options: A new browser for each test
A set of input rows; the linked test runs once per row with that row's values
The assertions the test will use
A report of which tests passed
questions.explanation: A @DataProvider returns rows of inputs. The @Test bound to it executes once for each row, receiving that row's values as method parameters.

questions.prompt: How do you link a @Test method to a @DataProvider named "logins"?
questions.explanation: You reference the provider by name on the test annotation: @Test(dataProvider = "logins"). The test's parameters then match the columns the provider returns.

questions.prompt: A @DataProvider returns four rows. How many times does the linked @Test method run?
options: Four times — once per row, each reported as its own result
Once, using all rows merged together
It depends on how many assertions are inside
questions.explanation: The test runs once per row, so four rows produce four executions, each appearing as a separate result in the report.

questions.prompt: Why is separating test data from test logic worthwhile?
options: It makes the browser open faster
The data changes far more often than the logic, so new cases become new rows instead of copy-pasted methods
It is required for any test to compile
It removes the need for assertions
questions.explanation: The inputs (what to check) change often while the steps (how to check) rarely do. Keeping them apart means adding a case is just adding a row, leaving the logic small and unchanged.
